nativeRange.habitat: Marine, reef-associated; shallow coastal waters on coral and rocky reefs from roughly 1–40 m depth, sheltering in caves and crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small fishes, shrimps, squid, and other small marine animals; in captivity generally accepts meaty frozen foods such as silversides, krill, and shrimp offered on a feeding stick. Typically fed two to three substantial meals per week rather than daily.

summary: A large Indian Ocean moray reaching around 65 cm, with a lilac to grey body marbled in brown and rows of dark dots on the head; generally considered an advanced-only species requiring a very large, escape-proof system.
careNotes: Gymnothorax griseus is the moray most often sold in the trade as 'Red Sea Moray Eel' on account of its distribution centred on the Red Sea, Arabian Sea and Persian Gulf; the more widely used common name in literature is 'Geometric Moray'. Note that one major retailer (LiveAquaria) lists its 'Red Sea Moray Eel - Juvenile' under the scientific name Gymnothorax javanicus (Giant Moray); that retailer entry appears to be a mis-identification, since G. javanicus reaches ~3 m and is not endemic to the Red Sea in the way G. griseus is. This entry follows the FishBase / aquainfo / Reeflex identification of the trade name 'Red Sea Moray' as G. griseus. Tends to be primarily nocturnal and reclusive, spending the day in caves and crevices and emerging at dusk to hunt. Has been reported to live up to ~30 years in the wild; captive lifespan is generally shorter. Like other large morays, this species is widely considered an escape specialist — a tightly-fitting, gap-free aquarium cover is generally regarded as essential. Feeding is best done with a feeding stick rather than by hand, given its powerful jaws and a bite that has been known to cause infection. Strong biological filtration and an oversized protein skimmer are generally advised given the heavy waste load of a large carnivore. Sources roughly agree on the husbandry envelope: aquainfo and Reeflex both cite a minimum tank of ~2000 L for adults, which is consistent with the ~65 cm adult size and the common 'tank length ≥ 3× fish length' rule of thumb for morays. Temperature filed as 24–28.5°C is averaged from FishBase environmental modelling (24.6–29.3°C), aquainfo (24–28°C), and Reeflex (24.6–29.3°C). Generally not considered reef-safe — will readily consume small fish, shrimp and small crustaceans.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the originally-assigned LiveAquaria 'Red Sea Moray Eel - Juvenile' page identifies the fish as Gymnothorax javanicus (Giant Moray), which conflicts with the target scientific name Gymnothorax griseus; FishBase identification of the 'Red Sea Moray / Geometric Moray' as G. griseus is the canonical record used here. Source of taxonomy, native range, max size, temperature envelope, and diet/trophic level.

additional.name: AquaInfo (aquainfo.nl)
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (65 cm), minimum tank size (≥2000 L), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), family Muraenidae, native range (Western Indian Ocean incl. Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf), nocturnal/reclusive temperament, and the not-reef-safe assessment. Also cited 'up to 30 years' wild lifespan.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Independent confirmation of 2000 L minimum tank, 65 cm max length, 24.6–29.3°C temperature envelope, Muraenidae/Anguilliformes taxonomy, Indo-Pacific (Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Indian Ocean, Madagascar, Mauritius, Kenya, Tanzania) range, advanced-only difficulty, and escape-risk warning. Used as the third source for cross-source averaging on tank size and temperature.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Originally assigned primary; demoted to additional because the LiveAquaria page identifies its 'Red Sea Moray Eel' as Gymnothorax javanicus rather than the target species G. griseus. Referenced here only to document the trade-name conflict in careNotes; husbandry numbers from this page (75 gal min tank, pH 8.1–8.2, SG 1.023–1.025, 74–84°F) were NOT used in the filed values because they describe a different species. The conflict itself is the most important fact captured from this source.
